speaker: interviewer
text: This is tape eight of the survivors of the Shoah interview with Itka Zigmuntowicz. It's March 3, 1996.

speaker: interviewee
text: There is a little poem that I wrote. It's one of my shortest poem. It's written February 10, 1978.

speaker: interviewer
text: What's it called?

speaker: interviewee
text: It's called 'My Love Belongs to All.' 'My body belongs to me. To God belongs my soul. To my children belongs my inheritance. My love belongs to all.' At the gate of Auschwitz, I didn't say goodbye to my family. But my mother's last words was, Itkalo, I have to go with the little children. I cannot leave them alone. But remember, my child, no matter what happen, don't become hateful and bitter. And I understand now, that when you hate others, when you're prejudice, you destroy yourself and others in the process. So I resisted. And my love belongs to all. I am outrage of what the Nazis did. But I will not let them destroy me by carrying hatred in my soul. Because you cannot be free when you hate others.

speaker: interviewer
text: So you were telling us about the hospital.

speaker: interviewee
text: When I came to the hospital, [PAUSES FOR 3 SECONDS] I felt very alone, very isolated, and very moved by the kindness of the people in contrast to what they did to us in the concentration camp. But at the same token, I didn't know a world of English. I was a stranger in a strange land. I wanted to preserve badly my own customs and traditions. And I had to learn a new language and everything anew. And I couldn't understand the language. There was the language barrier. And I realized how important languages are. Even in Auschwitz, if it wouldn't be that I knew when they gave an order, what it meant more or less, I would have beaten probably more. But I obeyed the orders because I knew if I don't, that I can be killed. And I have to thank God that they didn't choose me to do any job. I didn't want the privileges. I wanted to be-- just as long I didn't have to do anything to dehumanize myself. So I'm very grateful that I didn't was chosen for any of those kinds of jobs. And being there, I was thinking about this all. But the transition was terrible. And till I didn't go out from the hospital, I didn't realize it, because I was too sick. I only-- you know, the beginning. And then they sent us to another camp, DP camp in-- in-- it was called [SWEDISH] Also in Sweden. When I came to [SWEDISH], it was a quarantine. Because we were not-- they wanted to make sure that we don't carry diseases or anything. And the people would come, and just like to the zoo, look at us through the ways. They were kind. They didn't come with cruel. There was one family where came out, a mother and a father and a son, a young man. And I was always quick to learn languages. I always wanted to learn. I was-- always had a curious mind and wanted to learn. By the way, I never went to school here. Everything that I learned is with the seven grade public school. But I was studying all the time. I read poetry, and I read the philosophy, and I read the Bible, and the New Testament. My parents would kill me if they knew I read the New Testament. It was not allowed for a Yiddish [YIDDISH]. But, nevertheless, because I knew that knowledge is important, just the use of it. And when I came there, there was this young man. Attractive young man. And he-- they used to come. We would give them-- we'd get like 50 ores, which is like $0.50 here, to buy-- just pocket money. So I would give them the money. They would buy us the things that we, in mute language, we try to show them, they would buy it. And then one time when I learned how-- a little bit, this young man said to me, when I get out, he wants to take me out for a date. I didn't know who he is, what he was. And I agreed. And when we went out on the date, the first thing he said to me, you don't look like Jewish. And I felt like a knife twisted in my heart. I said, here, I suffered as a Jew, and I survived. And it's supposed to be a compliment that I'm not who I am. I didn't wait for him to say anything more. I started to run. And I ran away. And I said, no. Never will I see him again. Then there was in the-- in [SWEDISH], was a woman, a rabbi's wife, who survived. Her name was Rebbetzin Dukush. Rabbi's-- that's how we used to call it. Dukush was her name. But she was a rabbi's wife. So--

speaker: interviewer
text: How do you spell the last name?

speaker: interviewee
text: D-U-K-- D-U-K-U-S-H. S-C, or S-H, something like this here. And she wanted-- she was interested for the young children to continue to have a Yiddish education. So anyone who was under 21, she offered to take to a kosher lager. A lager is a camp, again, so-- but this was like a DP camp, still. So I volunteered.

speaker: interviewer
text: But trouble was, she would not take Bina. So I said to her, Mrs.-- Rebbetzin Dukush, how can you separate me-- this is-- this is to me like a sister. This is the only person in the world I have now. How can you separate us? She said, Itkalo, I cannot make exceptions. But I promise you. My-- my intention is not to separate you.

speaker: interviewee
text: She's-- and-- we going to bring her later. If we take her now, there will be other people who want to separate. And we must-- we cannot take anymore. We don't have room for all right now. So the two of you, I won't separate. But you must go first. OK.

speaker: interviewer
text: So we came. So because of this incident, I decided to go to this camp. I didn't want to be-- I said-- I want to be again in a community. And when I came there-- To--

speaker: interviewee
text: To [SWEDISH]. [SWEDISH], it's called. The place was called [SWEDISH]. There was a man, a Swedish man. And he couldn't speak any other language than Swedish. So he asked everybody, who speaks Swedish? Everybody start saying Itka. Because when I was already there, I started to get a dictionary and learn the words. Because I always realized how important it is, the country where you live, to learn the language. Because otherwise, you cannot communicate. I-- I realized this in the concentration camps.

speaker: interviewer
text: So everybody start saying, Itka. So he-- he picked me. And my job was supposed to be that-- you know, had to give out towels, linens, and things like this. You know. In the office, to work. But actually not office work. I didn't have any training for office work.

speaker: interviewee
text: And his name was Palm. P-A-L-M. [SWEDISH] Palm. He was in charge of this here. And it was a little kosher. It reminded me of my grandmother's, because Shabbat, you know, you prepared everything ahead of things. You didn't do anything on Shabbat. You know, even-- you know, I don't need to explain, because whoever understand the Jewish religion knows the prohibitions that we have. So-- and I felt very comfortable at that atmosphere. And it was young girls. And as she promised, little later, she brought-- she brought Bina there, which made me very happy. And then, from there, we decided that we have enough of collective life. Because what's happen at collective life, it's both good, positive, and negative. When you do something good-- something bad, one gets blamed for everybody. And I said, I was never on my own. Because from my parents' home, to the concentration camp, and now always somebody took care of me. So I said, it's time to get on our own feet. And Bina and I, and another girl, Regina Shapiro, we decided to go to our first job. And this first job is very important, to share this story. It was another test for me. And when we came there, it was a small village with-- with only three Jewish people there.

speaker: interviewer
text: What was the village?

speaker: interviewee
text: It was called [SWEDISH]. No-- no, [SWEDISH] was later. This was called Ravlanda. Near-- near Boras. Also it was belonging to Boras. At the time, I didn't know it's near Boras, but later I found out. And we-- we didn't have anything. Nothing. Because I had more on the day than I was born than on the day of liberation. All I has is my body and soul. Nothing else left. And as I mentioned, I didn't go into small details. But I didn't have anything. So-- not a photograph, not a heirloom, not-- nothing to show. Not a document. Not even a grave where I could go cry my heart out. Nothing.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when we came there, they gave us a few pieces of clothes. And we had room and board. So that was a good situation for us. And we were supposed to work. But the trouble was, I did-- I wasn't skilled in anything. I just knew the things that my family taught me at home.

speaker: interviewee
text: Anyhow, to make a short story shorter, we started to work there. And there were two-- two managers. One from the general manager of the hospital. Most of women who worked there were unmarried, elderly women. And this woman was also unmarried. And there was one in the kitchen where I was assigned. In the kitchen, for no apparent reason to me-- and I know by now you don't need a reason to hate somebody, because it's more to do with the person himself with the person that they hate. This woman hated me with a passion. If this good manager would say, make sandwiches. After she left, she said, scrub the floor. To-- always to-- I didn't object. Because I felt, I get paid. She asked me to do. I agreed to come. But one day was my half a day off. Here-- by the way, the good manager-- which I call the good, comparing to what I went through-- she would lend me books. She would teach me to write. She would invite me for a walk. So I already-- every little kindness, I no longer took for granted. One day, I was going to leave. It was my half a day off. And as I was leaving the door, she yells at me, don't go! Just like this. All of a-- all of a sudden, the Auschwitz, the kapos with the-- with the commandments, with the-- all the things flash before my husband-- my-- my eyes.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I very gently ask her, why not? She said, there are still dishes here. I said, there will always be dishes here. And I just walked out.

speaker: interviewee
text: A few minutes later, the good manager, as I called her-- I don't even know her name-- either this one or this. But I-- she was the manager from the general hospital, for-- it was for tuberculosis children, you know. Comes in. And she says to me, Itka, I heard what's happened. I know you're right. But do it for me. Go back. I was torn. My head told me, go, because after all, you're working here, and she's nice to you and everything. My heart says no. The beginning is the most important. If I give in now, then I am again a slave. Even though I'm free. And I said, no. I cannot do it to myself. And I started to go with her. First I went with her. Halfway. The kitchen was on top. We had the-- the lower level, you know. So I going. I felt, no, I can't do this. And I started to run. Tears were pressing to my eyes. And then I know-- I say, I cannot do it.

speaker: interviewer
text: When I came to the room, again the teaching of my family helped me. I was taught never make a long way serious decision when you're under emotional strain. So I talk to Sovek. Calmly I said, suppose I left already. Where will I go?

speaker: interviewee
text: I have no money. At least here, I have a roof over my head. I know I'm going to get a meal. If I leave this here, then I'm going to be completely alone. Beside, Bina and Regina is here.

speaker: interviewer
text: Regina, I didn't have that much connection at the time. We took her because she wanted to go and she was alone, so we felt, why not? But Bina and I was like sisters. So I said-- so I was very torn. So I said, put away your pride, Itkalo. And I went back to the manager. I said, listen. And I told her exactly how I felt. And I said, you can give me any kind of job. I'm not afraid of work. I-- I was in Auschwitz. I did all-- did the worst work that can, under the worst conditions, and under the worst thing with starvation.

speaker: interviewee
text: But let it be washing toilets. But I want to be asked to do something, not to order it. Especially when it's my time off. I would have gladly stayed if she would have asked me. I had nothing to do anyhow. There was no place to go. Nothing to do. And she said, I can't. She been working for me for 36 years. So I figured, uh oh. So it's more-- not what's justice, but what is comfortable for you. Then I didn't think anymore. Then nothing mattered anymore. Because there two things I cannot negate. I cannot give up. It's my life, my self respect, my freedom, and my menschlichkeit. That, I'm not going to give up for anybody in this world. Because without it, I no long-- I will exist, but I'm not going to be a mensch anymore.

speaker: interviewer
text: So at that point, I didn't think anymore. I thanked her, went into the room, kissed my friends goodbye, and left. When I came to the station, I didn't know where I'm going to go, what I'm going to do. But I just knew, whatever it's going to be, if I survived Auschwitz with menschlichkeit, I'm not going to negate myself now. And I left. I didn't ask the girls to come. They didn't offer to come. And I was glad that they didn't, because I didn't know where-- taking her away from here. And then I came to the largest big city. When I went to the train, they asked me, where do you want to go? I said, to the largest big city. That's how I found out they brought me to Boras. It was [YIDDISH] for me to come to Boras. And I came to-- to Boras. And fortunately, I found another survivor. And I was going and praying. I say, dear God, what do I do now? Where do I go? What-- I knew one thing. Any kind of work wouldn't be too hard. But as long it's decent. You know, I would not want to do anything which is not fit for a woman to do. And when she showed me where to go, you know, and I went to the unemployment office. They asked me, did I work before? I said, yes. Why did you-- and-- so why did you go away? I said, I wanted to get married. There is no boys. Which was not a lie. But I felt, she is Swedish, and-- and they are Swedish. And my intention wasn't to make them look bad. My intention was to preserve my-- my dignity, and not to be under conditions where I'm constantly give orders.

speaker: interviewee
text: What I didn't know-- that she's-- she said that I should come back. That she's going to call up there and check out my story. And she did, because at home, as I told you, I had no phone. Didn't have any of the modern gadgets we have today.

speaker: interviewer
text: And when I came back, she asked me, why did you try to protect her? I said, no. The truth is, eventually, I would have left. Because I'm a survivor. I suffered as a Jew. And I survived. I want to continue. I want to get married eventually, and there no young fellows there. So she said-- she gave me a job. And I said, I want a job for two other people. Because they are my friends. I explain the situation. And something about Sweden-- in Sweden, you could only get an apartment if you worked for the-- the person you worked for would provide you with an apartment. You stop working, you have no longer an apartment. So I got a job with the [INAUDIBLE], sewing. You know, where they made clothes for women. And I got an apartment. And after I had already lined up this, I notified Bina and Regina. They came back.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we were going. We were working together. But Bina and I shared everything except underwear. We didn't care mine, so use, or money. Regina was-- didn't quite-- she was a nice girl. I have nothing bad to say about her. But it was not the same relationship.

speaker: interviewer
text: So when I got the apartment, she had us-- a little apartment room alone. And the two of us had only one small room. In fact, I didn't even have a kitchen in it. But in the hallway was-- when you go down, I put a-- a little material. I made a drape. And I put in a hot plate.

speaker: interviewee
text: And from the sinks, from the regular sinks-- there was not a kitchen. I think I made a kitchen. That's how we lived. But after Auschwitz, that was heaven. I didn't complain. And we went to work. In-- in Sweden, the big meal is during the day. So every day, we would go, you know, for lunch. So we took shifts. One day, Bina went home and warmed up the lunch. One day, I. And I was always fast. And I'm rushing to go home to warm up the lunch. The homemade noodles that I made, with the chicken-- you know, like, still like home cooking, because I didn't even know that exist ready-made food at that time. And as I walk, bingo, I'm all on the ground. And somebody picks me up by the elbow from-- and I look up, and it's a man. And I thank him. And we start talking. And he tells me that he's also a survivor of the Holocaust. And his name is Abram Shmolevicz. And he lives on the same street. And he helps me home. And he was going to bandage my knees. I was still a modest girl, even after Auschwitz. So I says, oh, no. And I cleaned up my knees. And I warmed up the food. And he says to me, oh, smells so good. I would give-- gladly pay to get a meal like this.

speaker: interviewer
text: So I said to him, I don't run a restaurant. But on Sunday, when we are home, you're welcome to have-- I heard he's a survivor. You come up and have dinner.

speaker: interviewee
text: And he was-- start to-- I don't know if I should call it date-- but he liked me. And he came up. And I like him till this day. Thank God, he's alive. But as a friend. As a brother. No otherwise.

speaker: interviewer
text: And one time, he makes-- made a party. And he invited Bina and I to come. And I got very insulted, because I didn't know he inviting Bina too. And I said, what? Do you think I'm that kind of a girl? I'm going to go to a boyfriend's apartment? He says, shh, shh. I'm not inviting just you. I'm inviting Bina.

speaker: interviewee
text: And we came in. And as soon as I came in, a man was sitting there. At that time, I didn't know him. And he said in Polish to me, here is room. You can sit down. And from the minute I saw him, I liked him. And his name is Rachmil Zygmuntowicz, which is my husband now.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I was worried. Because we clicked it off just like this. Just like with Abram, I didn't have any emotional-- like contact, you know, like-- with Rachmil, immediately. So I was worried. What about Bina? Because we wouldn't separate. So he had a friend. And Rachmil was born in Belchatow. His friend, Mendel [POLISH]-- may he rest in peace, he's no longer alive-- was also from Belchatow. Shmolevicz was also from Belchatow. Since he saw that I am not going to marry him, he brought Rachmil and Mendel. And he later said, if you cannot be for-- for-- for me, let it be for my friend, you know. And he gave me a little bit of [YIDDISH]. It wasn't-- and we met. And I said to him, why don't you ask your friend to come too? He's going to take me home. So he took Mendel too. And then was a-- a funny little story. I don't know whether this fits in, but anyhow. He asked me if I knew a girl, Manya [POLISH]. And I knew her by sight. But I didn't know too much about anything. I said, yeah, I know who she is. And I said, why? So he told me that her brother's told me that he has a sister here. So I said, you should go and see her. And my girlfriend is doing like this to my arm. What, do you send a cat for butter? You sending him to another girl? You like him? So I said, no. I-- I do-- I insist he's going to go. So Bina would not permit him to go alone. She sent Mendel. And he went there. And he came back. He said, now we can engaged. And that was-- I met him June 24, 1964. July 1, we were engaged. What year?

speaker: interviewee
text: In 1964, the same year. And July 3, we were married.

speaker: interviewer
text: In what year was this?

speaker: interviewee
text: 1964. So from June 24, you know, just-- and-- and the funny point is, I never had a honeymoon. When I say somebody want me to hear about the honeymoon, everybody looks at me. What kind of crazy woman talk-- tells about her honeymoon?

speaker: interviewer
text: And what's happened in the honeymoon? Because they said, it was like in a small village. All survivors are home. They all saved up their sandwiches and the things to, you know, be able to make a little wedding. And the men were asking, so when is the train? You have to go by train. So they played a trick. And they let us miss the train. So all women slept in one house, in one room. And the men in the other. So we always joking about the honeymoon. After we-- How long after you met him did you get married?

speaker: interviewee
text: It must have been like 18 days or something like this. I didn't count the days. But from June 20-- 26-- 26, I think, or 24 or 26, till July the 13th. After July 1, I was engaged already.

speaker: interviewer
text: And what year was this again?

speaker: interviewee
text: The-- 1964. 1946! Oh, that's right. I-- because-- I-- 1946. 19-- 19-- in-- in 1945, we were liberated. This was in 1946.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you live?

speaker: interviewee
text: And-- what-- then what we did, Mendel had an apartment. They came both, and they got a little apartment. We had a little apartment, so we switched. He went over there. But it's something about this year, that was the happiest and unhappiest day in my life. Just like with the liberation. Because there was not one member of my family, not my-- one member of my husband's family. And at least he had two people from his own hometown. I didn't have a single one.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I remember it today. Somebody was joking. So how much you get in dowry? And my husband took my-- my-- my hand-- arm and showed. I'm getting 2567. See.

speaker: interviewee
text: We married especially during vacation time. Because we didn't have enough to live for the whole week. So we cooked a big pot of soup on our honeymoon, and the four of us shared. That was our honeymoon. And I borrow a dress in somebody else's home. We had nothing. And this how we started the life.

speaker: interviewer
text: And where did you-- and how long did you continue living there, in that town?

speaker: interviewee
text: In that town-- actually, we didn't-- till I came to America.

speaker: interviewer
text: When was that?

speaker: interviewee
text: Came in America in February 15, 1953. And when I came here, we already had two sons, thank God. And I went back to school. Not back, because I never went to this school. Because I went to a fashion design school. I earned a diploma. And I became a fashion designer. And I worked. And I said, I'm already free physically. Now I have to be economically independent, too. And we-- another thing I remembered is that when I came to Sweden, and I thought to myself, if I would die in the street, nobody would know who I am, just like [GERMAN].

speaker: interviewer
text: So the things I'm going to strive for more is people. Friends. And when we left, we had a lot of friends. Now-- excuse me.

speaker: interviewee
text: Another thing. When I went-- [DRINKS] I'm telling this story for a special reason. Because I want to show you that the Holocaust is over, but our lives are still affected by it. Not too many women go on a bicycle to deliver their son, their child. When I was in the-- when I was in Sweden, I became pregnant. And when I-- the whole time, we rehearsed how my husband is going to help me to go to the hospital, and everything else. It so happened that the doctor told me all the symptoms. But I didn't know anything about how a woman's body works, and how anything about birth, or anything like this. So he didn't tell me about a water bag. The morning after, my husband-- he worked in shifts from 5:00 in the morning till 2:00, and then from 2:00 till 11:00. That's how I could go to school, you know. So anyhow, it so happened that I left-- he left, and the water bag broke. And I didn't know what it was. I saw this is not normal, something normal for me. I went in to ask somebody. They said, I don't know. Ask the doctor. I didn't have parents. I didn't have anyone to ask. So the only means of transportation I had-- had no phone. Couldn't notify my husband.

speaker: interviewer
text: And I didn't know it's dangerous. So I went to the hospital, and I delivered my son. To deliver my child. And I came on the bicycle. And years later, when he ride-- rode a bicycle and I said, can I try? And he said, oh, Mom. I'm afraid at your age, if you gonna fall, you hurt. You're going to get hurt. And I said, Erland, you know that I went to deliver you on a bicycle? He was so amazed. And he wanted me to tell the story over and over.

speaker: interviewee
text: My-- so it affected everything. And we had nobody to share our joy. So the transition-- even though we were free-- but the transition, because still, I didn't-- till I was in the concentration camp, struggling to survive, we didn't realize. But we can-- came out. I'll never forget the first time when a family-- a Swedish family-- invited me to their home. Till then, I didn't realize it. And then I came. And they made a beautiful meal. They were the kindest people you could-- could ask. And as we were sitting in the meal, all of us, I was so happy for them. But all of a sudden, it hit me. I knew all the time that I didn't have-- I said, I'll never have this again. And I felt like I'm choking. Not to upset their dinner or everything. I run into the bathroom. I lock myself. And I started to cry. That was the first time I got a good cry after-- after liberation.

speaker: interviewer
text: I said, dear God. Everything is gone. A vanished world. You see why I wrote the poem, 'The Vanished World?' And this woman, there was a-- an elderly woman and her daughter and family.

speaker: interviewee
text: Was the kindest woman I met. She must have heard me cry, or she maybe assumed that I cried. She knocked at the door. She said, Itka, come out. You cried enough alone. Come share-- we'll be with you. And she embraced me. And I start to-- I said to myself, the only way to combat evil is to do good to it.
